output_text:
Restructured the section into the conventional IMRaD-style flow: setup → prior work → contribution → results, with the contribution paragraph rewritten so it stands on its own rather than back-referencing results that no longer precede it.

---

We consider stabilization of a partially observed linear time-invariant
plant under sensor scheduling. The dynamics follow x_{k+1} = A x_k +
B u_k + w_k, with w_k zero-mean Gaussian process noise of covariance Q.
At each step k the controller activates exactly one of m candidate
sensors, each yielding y_k^{(i)} = C_i x_k + v_k^{(i)} at communication
cost c_i. We assume (A, B) is stabilizable and that each pair (A, C_i)
is detectable, so that any single-sensor schedule admits a finite
steady-state Kalman filter.

Prior work on sensor scheduling has either solved the open-loop
selection problem via dynamic programming over the Riccati recursion
(Vitus et al., 2012) or relaxed it to a convex program over time-
averaged activation frequencies (Joshi and Boyd, 2009). Value-of-
information criteria from estimation theory have also been ported to
switched control settings (Krishnamurthy, 2016). These approaches
typically compute the schedule offline and assume the cost structure
is fixed a priori.

The principal contribution of this section is a closed-form one-step
lookahead rule, which we call regret-weighted EVPI, that selects the
sensor maximizing the expected reduction in LQG cost-to-go relative
to a cheapest-sensor default. The rule is defined in Eq. (14), admits
an O(m n^2) per-step implementation, and is fully state-feedback
rather than open-loop. We further establish, in Proposition 4, that
under bounded process noise it yields an exponentially stable closed-
loop with explicit decay rate rho < 1 depending only on (A, B, Q) and
the minimum sensor cost. Together, Eq. (14) and Proposition 4 are
what distinguish the method from standard certainty-equivalence
control, to which it otherwise collapses.

On a four-tank benchmark (Johansson, 2000) and on a quadrotor
altitude-hold task with three candidate altimeters, our method
achieves a 23% reduction in mean tracking error against the round-
robin baseline and a 17% reduction against the greedy variance-
minimization rule of Mo et al. (2014), under matched communication
budgets. Closed-loop trajectories remained bounded across all 200
randomly initialized trials, and the empirical cost-to-go matched
the LQG lower bound to within 4% on the linearized four-tank model.
A representative disturbance-rejection trajectory is shown in
Figure 3, and Table 2 reports the per-budget breakdown.

---

Notes on what changed and why:
- Swapped the order of the original third (results) and fourth (contribution) paragraphs so the reader meets the rule before its empirical numbers.
- Removed the back-pointer "tabulated in the preceding paragraph" — once the results follow the contribution, that phrasing no longer fits; replaced with a forward-neutral statement of what the rule and proposition do.
- Folded "Eq. (14)" into the sentence introducing the rule rather than reintroducing it at the end of the paragraph, which previously read as a second announcement of the same object.

Uncertainty: I assumed the surrounding paper follows a results-after-contribution convention; if this section appears in a venue or template (e.g., some applied-control journals) that front-loads empirical headline numbers, the original ordering may be preferable and only the cross-paragraph reference ("tabulated in the preceding paragraph") would need fixing.
